topics.title: MySQL Stored Procedures & Triggers — Encapsulated DB Logic & Audit
topics.description: Stored procedures are pre-compiled, parameterised SQL programs stored in the database that accept IN/OUT parameters and encapsulate loops, conditionals, and cursors. They reduce network round-trips for iterative operations. Triggers automatically execute SQL before or after INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE on a table — used for guaranteed audit logging, data validation, and maintaining denormalised counts. Both run inside the DB engine. BEFORE triggers can modify NEW column values (validation/normalisation). AFTER triggers see the committed row and write to audit/log tables. MySQL uses DELIMITER $$ to define multi-statement objects since the default ; delimiter conflicts with statement terminators.
topics.story: A stored procedure is a vending machine recipe encoded at the factory: you press a button (CALL procedure_name()), the machine runs the validated, pre-compiled recipe and returns the result — no manual SQL assembly, no repeated round trips. A trigger is the silent alarm on a safe drawer: every time someone inserts a row (opens the drawer), a second invisible action fires automatically without being called. You can't forget to call a trigger — it's enforced by the database engine itself.
topics.analogy: Stored Procedure: CALL name(IN p1, OUT p2) — like a Java method stored in the DB. DELIMITER $$  required to define the body without ending the statement. Cursor = pointer iterating a result set row by row inside the procedure. OUT parameter = return value declared separately. BEFORE INSERT trigger: can change NEW.column_value before the row is written (validation). AFTER INSERT trigger: row is committed, write to audit_log (cannot change the committed row). OLD = row state before change. NEW = row state after change (available in INSERT/UPDATE). DELETE trigger: OLD is available, no NEW.
topics.memoryAnchor: Stored Procedure: CREATE PROCEDURE name(IN a INT, OUT b INT) BEGIN...END$$ CALL name(1, @result); SELECT @result;. Trigger: CREATE TRIGGER tname BEFORE/AFTER INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE ON table FOR EACH ROW BEGIN...END$$. OLD.field = value before change. NEW.field = value after/during change. DELIMITER $$ required for multi-statement definitions. Benefits: fewer round trips, access control (GRANT EXECUTE). Drawbacks: hidden logic, hard to version, no unit tests.
topics.firstPrinciples: Stored procedures execute entirely inside the DB engine — no network hop per row processed. For operations processing 10K rows with conditional logic, this avoids 10K individual query round trips. Triggers are synchronous and atomic with the triggering statement — AFTER INSERT trigger writing to audit_log either succeeds with the INSERT or both roll back together in the same transaction. This is the only way to guarantee audit logging that cannot be bypassed by application bugs. MySQL triggers cannot fire other triggers (no cascading) and cannot call COMMIT or ROLLBACK.
topics.bruteForce: Application code fetches all rows to process, applies logic in Java, inserts results via N separate queries — each requiring a network round trip. Audit logging done in application code after every DB write — but application bugs, crashes between write and audit call, or direct DB access bypass the audit completely.
topics.optimizedApproach: Stored procedures for batch operations on large datasets (paginated reports, data migrations, complex statistical aggregations). AFTER INSERT/UPDATE triggers for guaranteed audit logging that cannot be bypassed from any access path. BEFORE INSERT trigger for data normalisation (uppercase names, set default timestamps). For complex business logic prefer app code (testable, CI/CD deployable) — only use procedures when network round-trip cost is measurable.
topics.whenToUse: Stored procedures: DB migrations processing millions of rows, complex calculations that would require hundreds of individual queries, shared logic accessed by multiple application stacks. Triggers: mandatory audit log (SOX, GDPR compliance), maintaining denormalised counters (post_count on users table), cross-row validation that single-row constraints cannot express. Avoid both when: logic needs unit testing, independent deployment, or when a single application owns the DB.

examples.title: Stored Procedure — Paginated Leaderboard with OUT Parameter
examples.explanation: DELIMITER changes the statement terminator from ; to $$ so MySQL doesn't try to execute each line inside the procedure as a separate statement. OUT parameters are MySQL session variables — call the procedure, then SELECT @variable to read the result. Cursors iterate result sets row-by-row; DECLARE CONTINUE HANDLER FOR NOT FOUND catches the end-of-cursor signal. Cursor-based loops are significantly slower than set-based SQL — use only when each row needs different logic that cannot be expressed as a single UPDATE.
examples.realWorldUse: DevLearner admin panel: 'Award streak bonuses' button calls AwardStreakBonus(50) which processes all eligible users in one DB round trip. Without the procedure: the backend would fetch all users with streak >= 7 (1 query), then loop and UPDATE each user (N queries) + INSERT XP event (N queries) = 2N queries. With the procedure: 1 CALL, everything runs inside MySQL.

examples.title: BEFORE INSERT & AFTER INSERT Triggers — Validation & Audit Log
examples.code:
-- ── Audit log table ──────────────────────────────────────────────
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS audit_log (
    id          BIGINT PRIMARY KEY AUTO_INCREMENT,
    table_name  VARCHAR(64)  NOT NULL,
    operation   VARCHAR(10)  NOT NULL,  -- INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE
    record_id   BIGINT,
    changed_by  VARCHAR(128),
    old_data    JSON,
    new_data    JSON,
    changed_at  DATETIME(3)  DEFAULT NOW(3)
);

-- ── BEFORE INSERT trigger: normalise username ─────────────────────
DELIMITER $$

CREATE TRIGGER before_user_insert
BEFORE INSERT ON users
FOR EACH ROW
BEGIN
    -- Normalise: lowercase email, trim whitespace
    SET NEW.email    = TRIM(LOWER(NEW.email));
    SET NEW.username = TRIM(NEW.username);
    -- Prevent empty username
    IF NEW.username = '' THEN
        SIGNAL SQLSTATE '45000' SET MESSAGE_TEXT = 'Username cannot be empty';
    END IF;
END$$

-- ── AFTER UPDATE trigger: write to audit_log ─────────────────────
CREATE TRIGGER after_user_update
AFTER UPDATE ON users
FOR EACH ROW
BEGIN
    -- Only audit if XP or role changed (not every field)
    IF OLD.xp != NEW.xp OR OLD.role != NEW.role THEN
        INSERT INTO audit_log(table_name, operation, record_id, old_data, new_data)
        VALUES (
            'users',
            'UPDATE',
            NEW.id,
            JSON_OBJECT('xp', OLD.xp, 'role', OLD.role),
            JSON_OBJECT('xp', NEW.xp, 'role', NEW.role)
        );
    END IF;
END$$

-- ── AFTER DELETE trigger: soft-archive deleted users ─────────────
CREATE TRIGGER after_user_delete
AFTER DELETE ON users
FOR EACH ROW
BEGIN
    INSERT INTO audit_log(table_name, operation, record_id, old_data)
    VALUES (
        'users',
        'DELETE',
        OLD.id,
        JSON_OBJECT('username', OLD.username, 'email', OLD.email, 'xp', OLD.xp)
    );
END$$

DELIMITER ;

-- ── Test: trigger fires automatically on DML ─────────────────────
INSERT INTO users(username, email, xp) VALUES ('  Alice  ', ' ALICE@EXAMPLE.COM ', 0);
-- username stored as 'Alice', email as 'alice@example.com' (BEFORE trigger normalised)

UPDATE users SET xp = 500 WHERE username = 'Alice';
-- audit_log now has a row: old_data={xp:0}, new_data={xp:500}

SELECT * FROM audit_log;
examples.explanation: BEFORE triggers run before the row is written — NEW.field assignments change the value that gets stored. SIGNAL SQLSTATE '45000' raises a custom error, rolling back the INSERT. AFTER triggers run after commit — you can write to other tables but cannot undo the triggering statement. OLD is available for UPDATE/DELETE (the state before the change). NEW is available for INSERT/UPDATE (the state being written). Triggers are atomic with the DML — if the audit_log INSERT in the AFTER trigger fails, the entire UPDATE rolls back.
examples.realWorldUse: DevLearner compliance requirement: all XP changes must be auditable. With an AFTER UPDATE trigger, even direct SQL run by an admin gets audited — the application cannot bypass it. BEFORE INSERT trigger normalises email casing — ensures alice@example.com and ALICE@EXAMPLE.COM are stored identically, preventing duplicate account issues.

problems.title: Stored Procedure Simulator — Paginated Query
problems.description:
Use window functions to calculate a running total and moving average.

```sql
SELECT
  order_date,
  amount,
  SUM(amount) OVER (ORDER BY order_date) AS running_total,
  AVG(amount) OVER (ORDER BY order_date ROWS BETWEEN 2 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS moving_avg_3
FROM orders;
```

**Example:**
Amounts: [100, 200, 150, 300]
Running total: [100, 300, 450, 750]
Moving avg (3-day): [100, 150, 150, 216.67]

**Key insight:** `OVER (ORDER BY ...)` defines the window. `ROWS BETWEEN 2 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW` = last 3 rows including current.
problems.hint1: Sort the full user list by XP descending. Total count = full list size.
problems.hint3: Return a record Page(List<User> rows, int total, int page, int pageSize).

problems.title: Audit Trigger Observer
problems.hint1: Define a TriggerObserver functional interface with onEvent(operation, oldRow, newRow).
problems.hint2: AuditableTable keeps a List<TriggerObserver>. insert/update/delete methods fire all observers.
problems.hint3: AuditLogObserver appends 'op: field=old->new' strings to a list.
problems.testCases:
INSERT id=1
UPDATE id=1 xp:0->500
DELETE id=1
Audit entries: 3
problems.sampleOutput: All employees in dept 1 get 10% salary increase

problems.title: Cursor-Based Row Processor — Conditional Batch Update
problems.description:
Implement a trigger that logs every salary change to an audit table.

```sql
CREATE TABLE salary_audit (
  id INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY,
  employee_id INT,
  old_salary DECIMAL(10,2),
  new_salary DECIMAL(10,2),
  changed_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);

CREATE TRIGGER after_salary_update
AFTER UPDATE ON employees
FOR EACH ROW
BEGIN
  IF OLD.salary != NEW.salary THEN
    INSERT INTO salary_audit VALUES (NULL, NEW.id, OLD.salary, NEW.salary, NOW());
  END IF;
END;
```

**Key insight:** `OLD` and `NEW` are special row references in triggers. `AFTER UPDATE` fires after the row is changed.
problems.hint1: Iterate the list. For each user with streak >= 7, compute bonus = min(50 * (streak/7), 200).
problems.hint2: Update the user's XP. Track totalProcessed (users with streak>=7) and totalXpAwarded (sum of bonuses).
problems.hint3: Return a ProcessingResult(int processed, int xpAwarded, List<User> updated).
problems.testCases:
user:1 +100 XP (streak=14)
user:3 +50 XP (streak=7)
user:4 +200 XP (streak=35)
Processed: 3
Total XP awarded: 350
problems.sampleInput: UPDATE employees SET salary=80000 WHERE id=5 (was 70000)

topics.title: MySQL Partitioning, Replication & Backup — Scale, HA & Recovery
topics.description: MySQL table partitioning splits one logical table into multiple physical segments based on a partition key. RANGE partitioning (by date/year) is most common for time-series data — partition pruning skips irrelevant segments, reducing scans from billions to millions of rows. HASH partitioning distributes rows evenly across N partitions when no natural range key exists. MySQL replication streams binary log (binlog) changes from primary to one or more replicas asynchronously. GTID (Global Transaction Identifier) replication assigns a unique ID to every transaction, simplifying failover. Backup: mysqldump creates logical backups (SQL text). XtraBackup creates physical backups (binary files, hot-copyable). Point-in-time recovery combines a full backup with binlog replay to recover to any moment within the retention window.
topics.story: Partitioning is like dividing a 10-year filing cabinet into yearly drawers. Looking for January 2024 records? Open only the 2024 drawer. Without partitioning, MySQL reads every row in all 10 years to find 2024 data. Replication is like a court stenographer who records every word spoken (binlog) and sends a transcript copy to a second courtroom every second. The replica court performs the same actions, staying in sync. If the primary courtroom burns down (failure), the replica has a complete record and can take over.
topics.analogy: RANGE PARTITION: PARTITION BY RANGE (YEAR(created_at)) — each year is a separate physical file. Partition pruning: WHERE clause includes partition key → MySQL reads only matching partition. HASH PARTITION: PARTITION BY HASH (user_id) PARTITIONS 8 — rows distributed evenly by hash(user_id). Binlog: transaction log of all DML/DDL changes (binlog_format=ROW is safest for replication). GTID: each transaction gets global ID 'server-uuid:sequence'. Primary-Replica: async by default (replica can lag). Semisync: primary waits for at least 1 replica ACK before responding (near-sync). mysqldump --single-transaction: consistent snapshot backup for InnoDB. PITR: restore dump + mysqlbinlog --start-datetime --stop-datetime | mysql.
topics.memoryAnchor: RANGE PARTITION: by date/year for time-series tables. Partition pruning: queries filtered by partition key skip irrelevant partitions. HASH PARTITION: even distribution, no natural range. Binlog format: ROW (safe, verbose), STATEMENT (risky with non-deterministic functions), MIXED. GTID: gtid_mode=ON, enforce_gtid_consistency=ON — simplifies failover. mysqldump --single-transaction: consistent InnoDB backup. --all-databases for full backup. Binlog retention: binlog_expire_logs_seconds=604800 (7 days). PITR: full dump + binlog replay. XtraBackup: hot physical backup, faster restore for large DBs.
topics.firstPrinciples: Partition pruning works only when the WHERE clause includes the partition key with an equality or range condition. A query without the partition key reads ALL partitions — potentially slower than unpartitioned due to overhead. Replication lag occurs when replicas apply binlog events serially. Enable parallel replication: slave_parallel_type=LOGICAL_CLOCK, slave_parallel_workers=4 to use multi-threaded apply. GTID makes failover position-free: instead of specifying binlog_file + binlog_pos, the replica just says 'I have up to GTID X' and the new primary knows exactly what to replay.
topics.bruteForce: Single unpartitioned table with 500M rows, no replication. Full table scan for every date-range query. Single primary with no backup — one disk failure loses all data. Manual mysqldump once a week during a maintenance window.
topics.optimizedApproach: RANGE PARTITION by month for tables > 50M rows with time-based queries (logs, events, submissions). Partition management: monthly job adds next partition (ALTER TABLE ADD PARTITION), archives old partitions (ALTER TABLE DROP PARTITION or EXCHANGE PARTITION). GTID async replication with at least one replica. Automated daily mysqldump + 7-day binlog retention = PITR to any second in the last week. Weekly validation: restore backup to a test instance and verify integrity.
topics.whenToUse: Range partitioning: append-only time-series tables > 100M rows (events, logs, orders by date). Hash partitioning: even distribution needed, no natural range key. Avoid partitioning: tables < 10M rows (overhead > benefit), frequent cross-partition joins (pruning only helps single-table scans). Replication: always for production (read scaling + HA). GTID: new setups — simpler failover. mysqldump: DBs < 50GB (logical, portable). XtraBackup: DBs > 50GB (faster, binary). Binlog retention: 7 days minimum for PITR.

examples.title: RANGE Partitioning — Submissions Table by Year
examples.code:
-- ── Create partitioned table by year ────────────────────────────
CREATE TABLE submissions (
    id           BIGINT        NOT NULL AUTO_INCREMENT,
    user_id      BIGINT        NOT NULL,
    problem_id   BIGINT        NOT NULL,
    status       VARCHAR(20)   NOT NULL,
    code         LONGTEXT,
    execution_ms INT,
    created_at   DATETIME      NOT NULL,
    PRIMARY KEY (id, created_at)  -- partition key must be in PK for partitioned tables
) ENGINE=InnoDB
PARTITION BY RANGE (YEAR(created_at)) (
    PARTITION p2023 VALUES LESS THAN (2024),
    PARTITION p2024 VALUES LESS THAN (2025),
    PARTITION p2025 VALUES LESS THAN (2026),
    PARTITION pFuture VALUES LESS THAN MAXVALUE  -- catch-all for future data
);

-- ── Partition pruning in action ───────────────────────────────────
-- This query reads ONLY p2024 (partition pruning)
EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM submissions
WHERE created_at BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-12-31'
  AND user_id = 42;
-- EXPLAIN shows: partitions=p2024

-- Without partition key in WHERE → reads ALL partitions (bad!)
EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM submissions WHERE user_id = 42;
-- EXPLAIN shows: partitions=p2023,p2024,p2025,pFuture

-- ── Partition maintenance ─────────────────────────────────────────
-- Add next year's partition before it fills pFuture
ALTER TABLE submissions REORGANIZE PARTITION pFuture INTO (
    PARTITION p2026   VALUES LESS THAN (2027),
    PARTITION pFuture VALUES LESS THAN MAXVALUE
);

-- Archive old partition: swap to archive table (instant, no data copy)
CREATE TABLE submissions_2023_archive LIKE submissions;
ALTER TABLE submissions EXCHANGE PARTITION p2023
    WITH TABLE submissions_2023_archive;
-- p2023 is now empty in submissions, data moved to archive table

-- Drop archive partition (irreversible)
ALTER TABLE submissions DROP PARTITION p2023;

-- ── Check partition row counts ────────────────────────────────────
SELECT partition_name, table_rows
FROM   information_schema.partitions
WHERE  table_name = 'submissions'
  AND  table_schema = DATABASE()
ORDER  BY partition_name;
examples.explanation: The partition key (YEAR(created_at)) must be part of the PRIMARY KEY when using InnoDB with auto_increment. Partition pruning is automatic when the WHERE clause filters on the partition key — MySQL's optimizer identifies which partitions can contain matching rows and skips the rest. EXCHANGE PARTITION is the key operation for archiving: it swaps a partition with an identically-structured table in microseconds — no data copy. This lets you archive millions of rows instantly without blocking the main table.
examples.realWorldUse: DevLearner submissions table will grow to hundreds of millions of rows over years. RANGE PARTITION by year: most queries are for recent data (last 30 days) — partition pruning means queries scan 1/N of the data. Monthly cron job: add next month's partition, archive submissions older than 2 years via EXCHANGE PARTITION. Analytics queries with date filters become 10-100× faster.

examples.title: MySQL Replication Setup & Backup/Recovery
examples.code:
-- ── Primary: enable binlog with GTID ─────────────────────────────
-- In /etc/mysql/my.cnf on primary:
/*
[mysqld]
server-id                = 1
log_bin                  = /var/log/mysql/mysql-bin.log
binlog_format            = ROW          # safest — stores before/after images
gtid_mode                = ON
enforce_gtid_consistency = ON
binlog_expire_logs_seconds = 604800     # 7 days binlog retention
*/

-- ── Create replication user on primary ───────────────────────────
CREATE USER 'replicator'@'replica-host' IDENTIFIED BY 'StrongPass123!';
GRANT REPLICATION SLAVE ON *.* TO 'replicator'@'replica-host';
FLUSH PRIVILEGES;

-- ── Replica: connect to primary ──────────────────────────────────
-- In /etc/mysql/my.cnf on replica:
/*
[mysqld]
server-id                = 2
gtid_mode                = ON
enforce_gtid_consistency = ON
read_only                = ON           # replica is read-only
slave_parallel_type      = LOGICAL_CLOCK
slave_parallel_workers   = 4            # multi-threaded apply
*/

CHANGE MASTER TO
    MASTER_HOST     = 'primary-host',
    MASTER_USER     = 'replicator',
    MASTER_PASSWORD = 'StrongPass123!',
    MASTER_AUTO_POSITION = 1;  -- GTID mode: no binlog file/pos needed

START SLAVE;
SHOW SLAVE STATUS\G
-- Check: Seconds_Behind_Master = 0 → replica is caught up

-- ── Backup: mysqldump logical backup ─────────────────────────────
/*
# Consistent InnoDB snapshot (no table locks)
mysqldump \
  --single-transaction \
  --quick \
  --routines \
  --triggers \
  --events \
  --all-databases \
  --master-data=2 \
  -u root -p > backup_$(date +%Y%m%d_%H%M%S).sql
# --master-data=2: records binlog position as a comment in the dump
# Used as starting point for PITR binlog replay
*/

-- ── PITR: restore to specific timestamp ──────────────────────────
/*
# Step 1: restore full dump
mysql -u root -p < backup_20240115_020000.sql

# Step 2: replay binlog from backup's GTID/position to target time
mysqlbinlog \
  --start-datetime='2024-01-15 02:00:00' \
  --stop-datetime='2024-01-15 11:59:00'  \
  /var/log/mysql/mysql-bin.000001 | mysql -u root -p
# Database is now at exact state as of 2024-01-15 11:59:00
*/
examples.explanation: binlog_format=ROW is the safest replication format: it stores the actual before/after row images rather than SQL statements. Statement-based replication can diverge on non-deterministic functions (NOW(), UUID(), RAND()). GTID mode makes failover position-free: when a primary fails, replicas can automatically determine what they're missing and resume from the correct position. slave_parallel_workers=4 enables 4 parallel apply threads, significantly reducing replica lag under high write load. --single-transaction ensures the mysqldump is consistent without locking tables.
examples.realWorldUse: DevLearner production setup: RDS handles replication (managed service, no manual config). For self-hosted: primary in us-east-1a, replica in us-east-1b. Daily mysqldump at 2 AM (off-peak) + 7-day binlog retention. If a developer accidentally runs DELETE FROM submissions in production at 3 PM, PITR restores to 2:59 PM: losing 1 minute of data instead of 1 day.

problems.title: Partition Key Router
problems.description:
Use window functions to calculate a running total and moving average.

```sql
SELECT
  order_date,
  amount,
  SUM(amount) OVER (ORDER BY order_date) AS running_total,
  AVG(amount) OVER (ORDER BY order_date ROWS BETWEEN 2 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS moving_avg_3
FROM orders;
```

**Example:**
Amounts: [100, 200, 150, 300]
Running total: [100, 300, 450, 750]
Moving avg (3-day): [100, 150, 150, 216.67]

**Key insight:** `OVER (ORDER BY ...)` defines the window. `ROWS BETWEEN 2 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW` = last 3 rows including current.
problems.hint1: Each partition covers [previous_upper_bound, upper_bound). Store as list of (name, lowerBound, upperBound).
problems.hint2: A partition overlaps query [from, to] if partition.lower < to AND partition.upper > from.
problems.hint3: Sort partitions by lower bound. Return all matching partition names.

problems.title: Binlog Event Replayer
problems.hint1: Use Map<Integer, Map<String,Object>> as the in-memory table (keyed by row id).
problems.hint2: INSERT: put(event.after). UPDATE: replace with event.after. DELETE: remove by event.before.id.
problems.hint3: Track highest applied GTID. appliedCount() returns total events applied.
problems.testCases:
Rows: 1
Events applied: 4
Alice xp: 800
Bob exists: false
problems.sampleOutput: All employees in dept 1 get 10% salary increase

problems.title: Point-in-Time Recovery Simulator
problems.description:
Implement a trigger that logs every salary change to an audit table.

```sql
CREATE TABLE salary_audit (
  id INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY,
  employee_id INT,
  old_salary DECIMAL(10,2),
  new_salary DECIMAL(10,2),
  changed_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);

CREATE TRIGGER after_salary_update
AFTER UPDATE ON employees
FOR EACH ROW
BEGIN
  IF OLD.salary != NEW.salary THEN
    INSERT INTO salary_audit VALUES (NULL, NEW.id, OLD.salary, NEW.salary, NOW());
  END IF;
END;
```

**Key insight:** `OLD` and `NEW` are special row references in triggers. `AFTER UPDATE` fires after the row is changed.
problems.hint1: Deep-copy the backup state as the starting point. Skip events with timestamp <= backup timestamp.
problems.hint2: Apply events in order where event.timestamp > backupTime AND event.timestamp <= targetTime.
problems.hint3: Return the restored state map. Events after targetTime are NOT applied.
problems.testCases:
Rows at 11:00: 2
Alice xp at 11:00: 500
Bob exists at 11:00: true
problems.sampleInput: UPDATE employees SET salary=80000 WHERE id=5 (was 70000)
